subject: ap-government
title: SCOTUS Comparison — Hernandez v. Texas (1954)
stimulus:
In the 1950s, Pete Hernandez, a Mexican American agricultural worker, was found guilty of murder and sentenced to life in prison by an all-white jury in Jackson County, Texas. Hernandez's defense claimed that people of Mexican ancestry had been discriminated against in Jackson County. They pointed to the fact that no person of Mexican ancestry had served on a jury in 25 years and that the Jackson County Courthouse itself practiced segregation in its facilities. The five jury commissioners, who selected the members of the grand jury, testified under oath that they selected jurors based only on their qualifications and did not consider race or national origin in their decisions.

In the ensuing case, Hernandez v. Texas (1954), the Supreme Court unanimously ruled in favor of Hernandez, deciding that evidence of discrimination against Mexican Americans existed in Jackson County and that the Constitution prohibits such discrimination.

parts.prompt: Identify the clause in the Fourteenth Amendment that was used as the basis for the decision in both Brown v. Board of Education (1954) and Hernandez v. Texas (1954).
parts.scoring_notes: Only the Equal Protection Clause earns the point. The Due Process Clause does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Identify the clause in the Fourteenth Amendment that was used as the basis for the decision in both Brown v. Board of Education (1954) and Hernandez v. Texas (1954).

parts.prompt: Explain how the facts in both Brown v. Board of Education and Hernandez v. Texas led to a similar decision in both cases.
parts.scoring_notes: The first point requires a specific fact from Brown (not the holding or reasoning). The second point requires connecting facts from BOTH cases to explain the similar outcome. Accept any accurate description of how systematic discrimination in each case violated the Equal Protection Clause.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in both Brown v. Board of Education and Hernandez v. Texas led to a similar decision in both cases. (1 point — describe relevant fact/holding about the required Supreme Court case)

required_elements: Brown was about segregated schools/racial discrimination in schools.
alternatives.correct_example: Brown was about segregated schools/racial discrimination in schools.

common_traps: Describing only one case without explaining the comparison does not earn the 2-point tier

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in both Brown v. Board of Education and Hernandez v. Texas led to a similar decision in both cases. (2 points — correctly explains how facts of BOTH cases led to different/similar holdings)

required_elements: In Brown, segregated schools led to discrimination against African American students, which was a violation of the Constitution/Equal Protection Clause. In Hernandez, discrimination against Mexican Americans in jury service was found to be a violation of the Constitution/Equal Protection Clause because it led to the conviction of Hernandez by a jury that excluded Mexican Americans.
alternatives.correct_example: In Brown, segregated schools led to discrimination against African American students, which was a violation of the Constitution/Equal Protection Clause. In Hernandez, discrimination against Mexican Americans in jury service was found to be a violation of the Constitution/Equal Protection Clause because it led to the conviction of Hernandez by a jury that excluded Mexican Americans.

common_traps: Describing only one case earns 1 point, not 2
Must connect facts to different outcomes

parts.prompt: Explain how an interest group could use the decision in Hernandez v. Texas to advance its agenda.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any plausible strategy an interest group could use to leverage the Hernandez precedent. The response must explain how the decision would be used, not just state that the group could use it.

scoring_points.description: Explain how an interest group could use the decision in Hernandez v. Texas to advance its agenda.

required_elements: An interest group could write amicus curiae briefs to encourage the Court to apply the decision from Hernandez in similar cases.
alternatives.correct_example: An interest group could write amicus curiae briefs to encourage the Court to apply the decision from Hernandez in similar cases.

required_elements: An interest group could lobby members of Congress to expand civil rights using the Hernandez case.
alternatives.correct_example: An interest group could lobby members of Congress to expand civil rights using the Hernandez case.

required_elements: An interest group could sponsor court cases encouraging the Court to apply the decision in Hernandez.
alternatives.correct_example: An interest group could sponsor court cases encouraging the Court to apply the decision in Hernandez.
